metadata.scope: Textbook-style matrix translations for worked examples.

entries.plainLanguage: Each row of A is one resource limit. Each column belongs to one product, and c stores the revenue per kilogram.
Column 1 describes one standard bag; column 2 describes one premium bag. The two rows encode the material limit and the premium-bag limit.
The single matrix row says x−80y≤0. If y=0, the row forces x=0; if y=1, it permits up to 80 units.
Q records the squared-load penalty. Its zero off-diagonal entries mean there is no cross-product term x₁x₂.

entries.dimensions: A is 1×2 · x and d are 2×1
entries.latex: A=\begin{pmatrix}1&-1\end{pmatrix},\quad b=\begin{pmatrix}1\end{pmatrix},\quad c=\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix},\quad A\bar x=b,\quad Ad=0,\quad c^{\mathsf T}d=2>0
entries.plainLanguage: The direction d lies in the null space of A, so moving along d preserves the equality. Its positive objective gain proves unboundedness.
Adding the two rows cancels x and leaves 0≤−2. The matrix combination exposes the contradiction immediately.
The variable order matters: every column of A and every entry of c follows (x,z⁺,z⁻,s).
The added column is 1 because one unit of slack represents one unused workshop hour.

entries.dimensions: A is 2×4 · basis matrix A_B is 2×2
entries.plainLanguage: The basis chooses columns 3 and 4. Because those columns form the identity matrix, the starting basic values are read directly from b.
The nonbasic reduced-cost vector is componentwise nonpositive. In a maximization problem, neither available nonbasic direction can improve the objective.
The identity block I creates the obvious starting basis u=b. Phase I then tries to push both auxiliary entries to zero.
The same column x₁ cannot produce two different right-hand sides. The auxiliary identity block measures the leftover mismatch; at least one auxiliary variable stays positive.
A_I keeps only the rows tight at the candidate. Two independent tight row normals pin down a point in two dimensions.

entries.dimensions: 4 inequalities in R² · 2 tight rows at the corner
entries.plainLanguage: At (3,2), the upper-bound rows are tight and independent. At an interior point, no rows are tight, so it is not a corner.

entries.dimensions: A is 2×2 · primal x and dual y each have 2 entries
entries.latex: A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix},\quad b=\begin{pmatrix}4\\5\end{pmatrix},\quad c=\begin{pmatrix}3\\2\end{pmatrix},\qquad (P)\;\max\{c^{\mathsf T}x:Ax\le b,\ x\ge0\},\quad (D)\;\min\{b^{\mathsf T}y:A^{\mathsf T}y\ge c,\ y\ge0\}
entries.plainLanguage: Transposing A swaps the primal roles: each primal row becomes a dual variable, and each primal column becomes a dual constraint.

entries.latex: A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},\quad b=\begin{pmatrix}4\\2\end{pmatrix},\quad c=\begin{pmatrix}3\\2\end{pmatrix},\quad A^{\mathsf T}y\ge c,\quad \bar y=\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix}
entries.plainLanguage: The two entries of y price the two rows of A. Aᵀy computes the resource cost of each product column.

entries.latex: A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&1\end{pmatrix},\quad s_P=b-A\bar x=\begin{pmatrix}0\\0\end{pmatrix},\quad s_D=A^{\mathsf T}\bar y-c=\begin{pmatrix}0\\0\end{pmatrix},\quad \bar y\odot s_P=0,\quad \bar x\odot s_D=0
entries.plainLanguage: Complementary slackness pairs entries component by component. The symbol ⊙ means multiply matching entries rather than multiply matrices.
The third resource has positive slack, so its matching dual price is zero. The vector product makes all three pairings visible at once.
Left-multiplying by (1,1) adds the two constraint rows. Integrality then lets us round the bound on x₁+x₂ down to 2.
Scaling the row reveals an integer left-hand side. Because that side is whole-valued, the fractional right-hand bound can be floored safely.
The transpose turns the active constraint’s row gradient into a column, so it can balance the objective vector in the KKT stationarity equation.

entries.dimensions: The 1-variable KKT system is a 1×1 matrix case
entries.plainLanguage: This is the same matrix stationarity rule as the higher-dimensional case; every vector and matrix simply has one entry.
